metadata.title: Why You Don't Diffract Walking Through a Doorway
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = quantum / wavelength visible; CRIMSON = classical / wavelength unmeasurably small
metadata.note: Exclusions: no Davisson-Germer numbers, no correspondence-principle formalism, no relativistic momentum.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged

swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Electrons diffract through crystals. Buckyballs make interference fringes. You walk through a doorway every day — and nothing happens. Quantum mechanics applies to you too. So why don't you diffract?

card.copy:
Why You Don't Diffract
Walking Through a Doorway
card.sub: de Broglie wavelength and the classical limit

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is Liam, in for Bear. Electrons diffract through crystals. Buckyballs make interference fringes. You walk through a doorway every day — and nothing happens

beats.narration_text: De Broglie said: a particle with momentum p has wavelength h over p. The same formula that describes an electron also describes a person — the only question is what number comes out.
An electron at fifty-four electron-volts has wavelength 0.167 nanometers — comparable to atom spacing. Diffraction happens. Why not for you?

card.copy:
Electrons: lambda = 0.167 nm — atom-sized. Diffract.
You at 1 m/s: lambda = ?
Why does the same formula not give you fringes?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: An electron at fifty-four electron-volts has wavelength 0.167 nanometers — comparable to atom spacing. Diffraction happens. Why not for you?

beats.narration_text: Plug in the numbers. A seventy kilogram person walking at one meter per second has momentum seventy kilogram-meters-per-second. De Broglie wavelength: six point six times ten to the minus thirty-four, divided by seventy — that is ten to the minus thirty-five meters.

Ten to the minus thirty-five meters. For comparison, a proton has radius ten to the minus fifteen meters. Your quantum wavelength is twenty orders of magnitude smaller than a proton. No slit, grating, or crystal anywhere in the universe has spacings this small.

For diffraction to appear, the wavelength must be comparable to the gap size. An electron fits through an atom-sized slit. You would need a slit as narrow as ten to the minus thirty-five meters — twenty times smaller than any known particle.

Quantum mechanics is not absent for you — it is present but the fringes are compressed into a spacing too fine to resolve. The wave function of a person walking exists and satisfies Schrodinger's equation. Its interference pattern has a fringe period of ten to the minus thirty-five meters.

Large molecules do diffract — just barely. A carbon-sixty buckyball has wavelength about two point five picometers, smaller than the molecule itself. Zeilinger's Vienna group made it diffract through a grating in 1999. The fringes were real, but the slit had to be only fifty nanometers wide.

The classical world is not a different regime of physics. It is the limit where the wavelength is so small that no available grating can reveal the wave structure. Quantum mechanics never stops applying — it just becomes undetectable as mass and speed grow.

Same formula for everything. Electron wavelength: atomic scale, diffraction visible. Human wavelength: ten to the minus thirty-five, no grating exists. Quantum mechanics never turns off — the fringes just become too fine to see.

card.copy:
lambda = h / p for everything.
Electron: 0.167 nm — diffraction visible.
Person: 1e-35 m — no grating exists.
Quantum never turns off.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Same formula for everything. Electron wavelength: atomic scale, diffraction visible. Human wavelength: ten to the minus thirty-five, no grating exists. Quantum
